key_insight: The paper proves that kernel MMD penalties can be embedded into mean-field games and trained efficiently with unbiased random Fourier U-statistics, yielding almost-sure convergence guarantees for the learned control policy.
engineering_relevance: This gives a scalable, theory-backed way to solve high-dimensional distribution-matching control problems such as Schrödinger bridges and coordinated EV charging with large-batch neural optimization.
method: Use RKHS/MMD penalties for both running interaction cost and terminal target cost.
Estimate penalties from finite samples via unbiased random Fourier U-statistics with linear batch complexity.
Parametrize the diffusion drift with a neural network and optimize by stochastic gradient descent.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments on Schrödinger bridge problems up to 100 dimensions and on an electric vehicle charging coordination task with heterogeneous vehicles.
watch_item: Performance and guarantees depend on coupled tuning of the penalty strength, random-feature count, sample size, and optimization tolerance, so the method may be sensitive to hyperparameter balancing.
paper_title: Kernel-based potential mean-field games with unbiased random Fourier $U$-statistics
